Released: 8 July 2026

!!!Note Deprecation of PGD Essential (PGD-S)
PGD Essential is no longer offered by EDB.
The PGD-S architecture will be removed in a future release of TPA.
!!!

!!!Warning Potential restart of Postgres on first deploy
Clusters that were previously deployed with unquoted boolean values (`on`/`off`/`yes`/`no`) in
`postgres_conf_settings`, Postgres will be restarted on the next run of
`tpaexec deploy` after upgrading to 23.44.1.

We recommend that you quote all such values, but changing this in a cluster that has already been deployed will not prevent this one-time restart.
!!!

This is a bug-fix release. The issues resolved in Trusted Postgres Architect 23.44.1 include the following:

## Enhancements

<table class="table w-100"><thead><tr><th>Description</th><th width="10%">Addresses</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td><details><summary>TPA now resumes an interrupted BDR 4 to PGD 5 upgrade instead of aborting.</summary><hr/><p>A BDR 4 to PGD 5 upgrade that was interrupted after the data nodes
reached PGD 5 but before harp-proxy was replaced with pgd-proxy used
to be misdetected as a minor PGD 5 upgrade on the next run, and would
abort trying to stop a pgd-proxy service that had never been
installed. <code>tpaexec upgrade</code> now recognises this half-migrated state
and resumes the harp-proxy to pgd-proxy migration, so a failed
upgrade can be recovered simply by re-running the command.</p>
<p>To make this reliable, reconfiguring a BDR 3/4 cluster to PGD 5 now
defaults <code>upgrade_components</code> to <code>postgres, pgd-proxy, pgdcli</code> (unless
you have already set it), so the upgrade migrates the data nodes,
replaces harp-proxy with pgd-proxy, and moves pgd-cli onto the PGD 5
line in the same run. These three components must be upgraded
together, and a major BDR 3/4 to PGD 5 upgrade that would upgrade
some but not all of them now stops before making any changes.</p>
<p>Upgrading pgd-proxy now also re-renders its configuration file and
systemd unit between installing the new package and restarting the
service, so a newer pgd-proxy that changes its configuration layout
or service unit starts cleanly against freshly rendered files.</p>
</details></td><td></td></tr>
</tbody></table>


## Bug Fixes

<table class="table w-100"><thead><tr><th>Description</th><th width="10%">Addresses</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td><details><summary>Fixed an issue whereby boolean values in <code>postgres_conf_settings</code> were written as <code>True</code>/<code>False</code> instead of <code>on</code>/<code>off</code>, causing enum-typed Postgres parameters to be silently ignored.</summary><hr/><p>YAML parses unquoted <code>off</code>, <code>on</code>, <code>yes</code> and <code>no</code> as booleans, so a
setting such as <code>bdr.default_streaming_mode: off</code> in
<code>postgres_conf_settings</code> reached TPA as a boolean and was written to
<code>postgresql.conf</code> as <code>False</code>. Postgres accepts that for genuine
boolean parameters, but rejects it for enum parameters whose valid
values include <code>off</code> or <code>on</code> (for example <code>bdr.default_streaming_mode</code>),
so the value was silently ignored and the parameter was left at its
default.</p>
<p>TPA now writes boolean values in <code>postgres_conf_settings</code> as <code>on</code> or
<code>off</code>, which Postgres accepts both as boolean values and as enum
labels. Quoting values exactly as they should appear in
<code>postgresql.conf</code> remains the recommended practice.</p>
<p>On clusters that used unquoted boolean values in
<code>postgres_conf_settings</code>, Postgres will be restarted on the next run
of <code>tpaexec deploy</code>.</p>
</details></td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td><details><summary>Fixed EPAS package resolution failures on RHEL 9.7 and later by removing the -server-devel package from the default EPAS package list.</summary><hr/><p>On RHEL 9 minor versions 9.7 and later, ABI-breaking changes mean the EPAS
-server-devel package now requires clang-devel/llvm-devel versions that
are not available in the repositories. As a result, <code>tpaexec download-packages</code> and package installation during deploy failed for EPAS
clusters on RedHat and SLES, with errors such as &quot;nothing provides
(llvm-devel &gt;= 20 with llvm-devel &lt; 21) needed by edb-asNN-server-devel&quot;.</p>
<p>The -server-devel package has been removed from the default EPAS package
list for RedHat and SLES. These packages are only needed to build Postgres
extensions from source and are not required by EDB products, so normal
deployments are unaffected. If you still need them, you can add them
explicitly via <code>postgres_packages</code> in the cluster configuration.</p>
</details></td><td>61967</td></tr>
<tr><td><details><summary>Fixed an issue whereby PEM server installation or upgrade could fail or leave the PEM application in a failed state.</summary><hr/><p>Changes made in TPA 23.44.0 to bump the versions of <code>edb-python</code> and
<code>edb-python-mod_wsgi</code> to meet the requirements of PEM 10.4 did not
ensure that the new WSGI module was enabled, resulting in the Apache
web server being unable to start on Debian-like platforms, where
explicit activation is required. This fix resolves that issue by
using the same fact for both package installation and module
activation. This fix also resolves a related issue where PEM server
upgrade could fail because the Apache configuration was transiently
invalid between the removal of an old WSGI module and the activation
of a new one.</p>
</details></td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td><details><summary>Fixed an issue whereby the pgbouncer TLS-protection test could fail during upgrade and test runs.</summary><hr/><p>The &quot;Ensure that connections through pgbouncer are TLS-protected&quot; task
connected to pgbouncer without an <code>sslmode</code> attribute. TPA configures
pgbouncer with <code>client_tls_sslmode = require</code>, which rejects any client
that does not initiate the SSL handshake. The task therefore failed with
<code>FATAL: SSL required</code> on every pgbouncer-bearing cluster whenever an
upgrade or <code>tpaexec test</code> run reached it, preventing upgrades from
completing.</p>
<p>The TLS check now connects to the pgbouncer administrative database with
<code>sslmode=require</code>. A successful connection is itself the proof that
pgbouncer accepted a TLS-required client, and the test no longer depends
on the operator's <code>pgbouncer_databases</code> containing a particular routable
backend dbname.</p>
<p>The same test also verifies pgbouncer's backend host and port by looking
up the <code>pgbouncer_auth_database</code> route in <code>SHOW DATABASES</code>. On clusters
where the operator's <code>pgbouncer_databases</code> doesn't include that route —
neither via the wildcard <code>*</code> nor an explicit entry — the lookup returned
nothing and the test crashed. It now skips that check gracefully when the
route isn't present, so upgrades can complete. To keep the check active
on a customised configuration, add an explicit <code>pgbouncer_auth_database</code>
entry to <code>pgbouncer_databases</code>.</p>
</details></td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td><details><summary>Fixed an issue whereby BDR/PGD deploys failed on SLES with an undefined-variable error while recording the primary BDR/PGD package name.</summary><hr/><p>Deploying a BDR or PGD cluster on SLES 15 aborted with &quot;The task includes
an option with an undefined variable. The error was: 'dict object' has
no attribute 'SUSE'&quot; while recording the primary BDR/PGD package name.
The tasks that record this package name looked it up by OS family
(&quot;SUSE&quot;) instead of by distribution (&quot;SLES&quot;), the key actually used in
TPA's package lists, so the lookup failed and the play stopped before
any BDR package was installed. These tasks now resolve the package name
the same way as the rest of the package selection, and SLES deploys
proceed as they do on RHEL and Debian.</p>
</details></td><td>62347</td></tr>
<tr><td><details><summary>Fixed an issue whereby Postgres SSL certificate selection was incorrect when services shared a node.</summary><hr/><p>When an <code>etcd</code>, <code>haproxy</code>, <code>pgd-proxy</code> or other TLS-enabled service
shared a host with Patroni and Postgres, TPA could configure Postgres
and Patroni to use another service's certificate and key instead of
their own. This was most visible on an M1 cluster with Patroni where
etcd ran on the database nodes: the replicas were given the etcd
certificate (<code>/etc/tpa/etcd/etcd.crt</code>), which the postgres user cannot
read, so Postgres failed to start. Setting the certificate paths
explicitly in <code>config.yml</code> worked around the Postgres failure but then
left the Patroni REST API using the same unreadable etcd certificate.</p>
<p>The certificate role no longer routes per-service certificate paths
through the shared <code>ssl_cert_file</code>/<code>ssl_key_file</code> variables, so each
service now uses its own certificate regardless of how roles are
distributed across nodes. The documented <code>ssl_cert_file</code> and
<code>ssl_key_file</code> overrides for the Postgres server certificate continue
to work as before; clusters that do not set them use the default
Postgres certificate at <code>/etc/tpa/&lt;cluster_name&gt;.crt</code>.</p>
</details></td><td>57402</td></tr>
<tr><td><details><summary>Fixed pem_web_server_name to point to the correct path in the pem.conf file.</summary><hr/><p>A bug was found in the pem.conf file where pem_web_server_name pointed
to an incorrect path. This has been corrected to ensure the right path
is referenced where it is needed.</p>
<p>Fix contributed by skrzyzok (<a href="https://github.com/skrzyzok">https://github.com/skrzyzok</a>).</p>
</details></td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td><details><summary>Fixed an issue whereby empty entries in an instance's role list produced a spurious Ansible inventory group.</summary><hr/><p>A dangling list item (a &quot;- &quot; with nothing after it) in an instance's
role list parsed to an empty value and was carried through to the
generated inventory, where it produced a spurious <code>role_None</code> Ansible
group. Such empty entries are now dropped, and provision emits a
warning so a stray &quot;-&quot; left over from hand-editing config.yml is easy
to spot.</p>
</details></td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td><details><summary>Fixed an issue whereby PEM servers were not excluded from cluster-replication tasks when EFM was the failover manager.</summary><hr/><p>When EFM was chosen as the failover manager, PEM servers were not
excluded from cluster-replication tasks, which could cause issues with
the replication topology and EFM role delegation during deployment.
PEM servers are now excluded from these tasks in that configuration.</p>
</details></td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td><details><summary>Fixed an issue whereby configuring a PEM agent against a PEM 10.5 server could fail because TPA passed pemworker options that PEM 10.5 no longer accepts.</summary><hr/><p>Two invocations were affected. <code>pemworker --register-server</code> was called
with <code>--cert-path</code>, which has never been a valid option for that
subcommand and is now removed unconditionally.</p>
<p><code>pemworker --enable-probe</code> was called with <code>--pem-user</code> and
<code>--pem-port</code>, which were silently required by PEM versions before 10.5
but are rejected by PEM 10.5 and later. TPA now omits these arguments
when <code>pem_agent_version</code> is 10.5.0 or newer, and continues to pass them
on older PEM versions where they remain necessary.</p>
</details></td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td><details><summary>Fixed an issue whereby database users were not synchronized to witness nodes during a BDR 4 to PGD 5 upgrade.</summary><hr/><p>During a BDR 4 to PGD 5 upgrade, the pgdproxy user and other database
roles created on data nodes were not propagated to witness nodes. This
happened because DDL replication does not reach witness nodes once
<code>node_kind='witness'</code> is set, and the upgrade path did not create the
roles locally on the witness the way a fresh PGD 5 deployment would.</p>
<p>The upgrade process now parts the witness after the data node upgrade
loop and rejoins it afterwards to sync the full catalog from the data
nodes, ensuring the witness has the same database user list as a fresh
PGD 5 deployment.</p>
</details></td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td><details><summary>TPA now rejects volume definitions placed in cluster_vars.</summary><hr/><p>Volumes are per-instance settings and must be defined under
<code>instance_defaults</code> (as <code>default_volumes</code>) or on individual instances.
When <code>volumes</code> or <code>default_volumes</code> were set in <code>cluster_vars</code> they
were silently ignored, which on AWS resulted in instances being
created with the default AMI root volume instead of the intended one.
TPA now detects this misconfiguration and fails early with a message
explaining where volumes should be defined.</p>
</details></td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td><details><summary>Added a deploy-time assertion that fails when pem_shared is set on an instance without the pem-server role.</summary><hr/><p>Previously this misconfiguration could pass silently and lead to
confusing downstream errors.</p>
</details></td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td><details><summary>Fixed an issue whereby <code>efm_user_is_superuser</code> did not default to true when omitted from the cluster configuration file.</summary><hr/><p>When <code>efm_user_is_superuser</code> was not present in a cluster's
configuration file, it failed to resolve to its documented default of
<code>true</code>, which could prevent redeployment of pre-existing clusters. The
variable now correctly defaults to <code>true</code> when it is not set.</p>
</details></td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td><details><summary>TPA now warns when PEM agents are configured without a PEM server.</summary><hr/><p>TPA now checks whether the configuration used to create the cluster
has a complete PEM role configuration whenever at least one pem-server
role and at least one pem-agent role are present in config.yml. To
allow a partial PEM configuration, add the <code>pem-role-check</code> task
selector to the list of excluded tasks in the cluster configuration
file; this bypasses the check and lets deployment continue with an
incomplete PEM configuration, which may expose the cluster to failures
later in the deployment.</p>
</details></td><td></td></tr>
</tbody></table>
